title: Dog Grey Gums and Labored Breathing: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog has grey gums and labored breathing, they are in critical distress. This is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate vet care.
content_html:
What do grey gums and labored breathing mean?

Grey or bluish-tinted gums, a condition known as cyanosis, are a sign that your dog is not getting enough oxygen into their bloodstream. When this occurs alongside labored breathing—which may look like gasping, excessive panting, or heavy abdominal movement—it indicates that the body's vital organs are being starved of oxygen. This can be caused by heart failure, lung disease, choking, or severe shock.

Is this an emergency?

Yes. This is a life-threatening emergency with a High Urgency level. You must seek immediate veterinary attention at the nearest open animal hospital. Do not wait for a scheduled appointment or attempt to treat your pet at home. Oxygen deprivation can lead to organ failure and death within minutes.

Why a photo helps with triage

If you are able to safely snap a quick photo of your dog's gums without delaying your departure, it can be a vital tool for the veterinary triage team. A photo provides an objective visual of the gum color at its worst, helping the staff prioritize your pet the moment you arrive. It also serves as a baseline to monitor if your dog's condition is improving or deteriorating during emergency treatment.

faqs.question: What is the normal color for a dog's gums?
faqs.answer: Healthy dog gums should be a bubblegum pink. Any other color, such as grey, blue, white, or deep red, is a sign of a medical problem.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog anything to help them breathe?
faqs.answer: No. Do not administer any medications or liquids, as this could lead to choking or worsen the underlying condition. Focus entirely on getting them to a vet safely.

faqs.question: How should I transport my dog in this condition?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as calm as possible and ensure they are in a position where their airway is not restricted. If they prefer to sit up to breathe, allow them to do so.
